coredata.dc:title: Preparation of coal-based graphene quantum dots/α-Fe2O3 nanocomposites and their lithium-ion storage properties
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yating
Zhang, Kaibo
Jia, Kaili
Liu, Guoyang
Ren, Shaozhao
Li, Keke
Long, Xueying
Li, Meng
Qiu, Jieshan
coredata.dc:description: Nano-Fe2O3 particles on a nickel substrate have been obtained by electrodeposition technique adjusting the ratio of electrolyte solvent (DMF and water), and then it was used as the working electrode to obtain the C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composite material via second-step electrodeposition with the coal-based graphene quantum dots (C-GQDs) solution which had been prepared from Taixi anthracite powder as the electrolyte. The lithium-ion storage performance of C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composites as the anode in the lithium-ion battery was studied, and the results show that the composites exhibited excellent cyclability and rate capability. When the current density was 1 A/g, the specific capacitance of C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composites was up to 1582.5 mAh/g, and it could maintain 1320 mAh/g after 110 cycles. The specific capacitance was 1091 mAh/g at a high current density (5 A/g).
coredata.dcterms:subject: α-Fe2O3
GQDs
Coal
Lithium-ion battery
Anode material

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We wish to thank Prof Y.T.Z., J.S.Q. and K.B.Z., K.L.J., S.Z.R., G.Y.L., K.K.L., X.Y.L., M.L. for their contributions during the experiment and paper writing process. All authors appreciate the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1703251 ) and the Key Research and Development Plan of Shaanxi Province (No. 2017ZDCXL-GY-10-01-02) via a Discovery Grant.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM and EDS images of samples under four experimental ratios:(a)∼(c) Fe2O3-0; (d)∼(f) Fe2O3-1; (g)∼(i) Fe2O3-2; (j)∼(l) Fe2O3-3.
(a) XRD images of Fe2O3-0, Fe2O3-1 Fe2O3-2 and Fe2O3-3; (b) Raman images of Fe2O3-2 and C-GQDs/Fe2O3-2.
(a) TEM images of C-GQDs (Inset is the particle size distribution obtained from the TEM images); (b) HRTEM images of C-GQDs (Inset is the SAED image).
(a) (b) SEM and EDS images of C-GQDs/Fe2O3-2.

(a) Cyclic voltammogram of C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3-2 at 0.1 mV/s;(b) Constant current charge-discharge curves of composite at 0.2 A/g; (c) Cycle performance curves of C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3-2, Fe2O3-0, Fe2O3-1, Fe2O3-2 and coulombic efficiency; (d) Rate performance curve of Fe2O3-2 and composite.
AC Impedance Spectroscopy of Fe2O3-2 and composite electrodes.
Proximate and ultimate analysis of Taixi coal.
Impedance parameters of C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3-2 electrodes for discharge-charge cycle.

head.ce:title: Preparation of coal-based graphene quantum dots/α-Fe2O3 nanocomposites and their lithium-ion storage properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi’an University of Science and Technology, Xi’an 710054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Xi’an University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Liaoning Key Lab for Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, Liaoning, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Key Lab for Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nano-Fe2O3 particles on a nickel substrate have been obtained by electrodeposition technique adjusting the ratio of electrolyte solvent (DMF and water), and then it was used as the working electrode to obtain the C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composite material via second-step electrodeposition with the coal-based graphene quantum dots (C-GQDs) solution which had been prepared from Taixi anthracite powder as the electrolyte. The lithium-ion storage performance of C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composites as the anode in the lithium-ion battery was studied, and the results show that the composites exhibited excellent cyclability and rate capability. When the current density was 1 A/g, the specific capacitance of C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composites was up to 1582.5 mAh/g, and it could maintain 1320 mAh/g after 110 cycles. The specific capacitance was 1091 mAh/g at a high current density (5 A/g).

ce:section.ce:para: The advantages of lithium-ion rechargeable batteries include high energy density, high operating voltage, long cycle life, absence of memory effect, and safe use. In recent years, lithium-ion rechargeable batteries have been widely used in portable appliances, electric vehicles, medical equipment, and other products [1,2]. Graphite is currently used as an anode material in commercial lithium ion batteries [3]. However, due to its low theoretical capacity (372 mAh/g) and safety issues, graphite cannot meet the growing demand for lithium-ion batteries in high-power equipment applications. Therefore, finding an environmentally friendly and safe anode material with a high theoretical capacity to replace graphite is an urgent issue.

The use of Fe2O3 as an anode material for lithium ion batteries is popular because it is environmentally friendly, abundant, and low cost. It has a high theoretical capacity (1007 mAh/g) [4–6]. For example, Shakir et al. [7] designed a porous Fe2O3 aerogel structure encapsulated in three-dimensional graphene, and its reversible specific capacitance could reach 1129 mAh/g after 130 cycles at a current density of 0.2 A/g. Lee [8] reported a RGO/α-Fe2O3 hollow nanorod composite material—the composite material was synthesized through a one-step microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, and the unique porous structure showed excellent rate capability and cyclability. However, the Fe2O3 anode material exhibits poor electrical conductivity, and the changes of the volumetric expansion during the charge and discharge process will break the crystal structure. The changes during the insertion and extraction of lithium ions result in poor contact between the active material and the current collector. Consequently, the cycle life of the electrode is affected, leading to capacity loss and decreased electrochemical performance [9]. Researchers have attempted to solve the problem by constructing a graphene quantum dots conductive network and a unique nano-skeleton.

Taixi anthracite is a high-carbon mineral that is common in nature and has low ash, low phosphorus, low sulfur, high degree of graphitization, and good thermal stability [10–13]. Coal-based graphene quantum dots (C-GQDs) were prepared via an acid oxidation method from Taixi anthracite powder, and two-step electrodeposition was used to prepare a self-supporting and binder-free C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composite electrode through a simple preparation process. This composite electrode could solve issues of hindered ion transfer, decreased electric conductivity, and apparent capacity loss due to the addition of a binder during the preparation of conventional electrodes. In addition, this new high-efficiency anode material for lithium-ion batteries was fabricated using a unique spatial structure of nano-Fe2O3 as well as an electrically conductive network possessing the “bridge effect” of C-GQDs. And this approach expands a way of high valued-added using of anthracite.

Here, anthracite (Taixi coal from Ningxia of China) was used as the raw material; coal dust (200 mesh) was obtained after pulverization and deashing. To a round bottom flask, 100 mg of sample was added followed by 70 mL of nitric acid (6 mol/L) and ultrasonic treatment for 2 h. An Allihn condenser was installed on the top of the round bottom flask and oxidization was performed in an oil bath at 140 °C for 24 h. The mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature, and sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) was added to adjust the pH to 7. The mixture was then filtered using 0.022 μm filter paper to give a yellow colloidal supernatant, i.e., a solution of C-GQDs [14,15]. Nitrate was removed by dialysis (1000 Da) for three days, and characterization and elemental analysis results are shown in Table 1 .

To a beaker, 0.7 g of (NH4)2C2O4 and 0.45 g of FeCl3 were added and dissolved by adding water and N, N -dimethylformamide (DMF) with a total volume of 50 mL. To investigate the effect of the mixing ratio of DMF and water, four ratios were selected (VDMF:Vwater = 0:1, 1:1, 2:1, and 3:1). Nickel foam was sonicated for 20 min with acetone, deionized water, and ethanol, respectively. Electrodeposition was performed for 30 min (constant current electrodeposition, current density: 7.8 mA/cm2) using dried nickel foam as the working electrode, a platinum sheet as the counter electrode, and a mixed solution as the electrolyte. The electrodeposited nickel foam was placed in a tube furnace, and the temperature was increased from room temperature to 500 °C at a heating rate (3 °C/min), and held for 2 h (air atmosphere) to give a series of nickel-based Fe2O3 samples. The samples obtained at VDMF:Vwater = 0:1, 1:1, 2:1, and 3:1 were recorded as Fe2O3-0, Fe2O3-1, Fe2O3-2, and Fe2O3-3, respectively.

Electrodeposition was conducted for 10 min with a solution of C-GQDs as the electrolyte and the nickel-based Fe2O3 samples as the working electrode: constant potential electrodeposition voltage: 6 V; counter electrode: platinum electrode. Finally, the C-GQDs/Fe2O3 composite material was obtained. The preparation process is shown in Fig. 1 .

The crystal structure of the material was characterized by X-ray diffractometer (MiniFlex 600, Cu target, Kα radiation as radiation source, Rigaku, Japan). An inVia Reflex Raman micro-spectrometer (Renishaw, UK) was used with 10% laser at 525 nm as the excitation light source. The micromorphology and structure of the samples were observed using an S-4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM) (HITACHI, Japan) and an H-7650 transmission electron microscope (HITACHI, Japan). The adsorption-desorption isotherms for N2 were recorded by using Quantachrome SI-21 (USA). FTIR spectra were measured on a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Spectrum GX, USA). AMETEK's PASTAT MC electrochemical workstation was used for cyclic voltammetry analysis. The CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd.) was used for electrochemical impedance testing.

The electrode material and lithium sheet were used as the counter electrode, polypropylene film (Celgard 2400) was used as the separator, and 1 mol/L lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6) in 1:1 (volume ratio) ethylene carbonate (EC)-dimethyl carbonate (DMC) was used as the mixed electrolyte. The button battery (water and oxygen contents were controlled below 0.1 ppm) was assembled in the MBRAUN UNllab Plus glovebox filled with argon, and the system was allowed to stand for 12 h at room temperature. A constant current charge and discharge tests were performed in a LAND CT2001A multichannel battery testing system.

Fig. 2 shows the SEM and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) images of the series of nickel-based Fe2O3 samples. Fig. 2 shows that the size and morphology of Fe2O3 samples prepared under different preparation conditions were drastically different. As shown in Fig. 2a-b, Fe2O3-0 samples were bulk particles and 1 to 2.2 μm in size. With increasing amounts of DMF, the Fe2O3-1 sample became smaller and more porous (Fig. 2d-e). Fe2O3-2 was obtained with more DMF added to the electrolyte. Fig. 2g-h shows that the sample has a flaky and antler-shaped skeleton structure. This unique structure has sufficient three-dimensional pore space to accommodate the change in volume expansion—this expected to solve the issue of volume changes in the charge-discharge process when used as an anode material. (Fig. 2c, f, i, and l) are EDS images of Fe2O3-0, Fe2O3-1, Fe2O3-2, and Fe2O3-3 samples. The Fe and O elements were found in (Fig. 2c, f, i). No Fe element was found in Fig. 2l. This may be because when the DMF:water ratio was 3:1, excessive amounts of DMF surfactant were adsorbed on the surface active sites. Therefore, the growth rate of the crystal decreased [16,17], and no Fe2O3 was generated.

Fig. 3 a shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Fe2O3 grown under four experimental conditions. Strong diffraction peaks were detected for Ni (44.54°, 51.81°, and 76.34°) because the substrate was nickel foam. The diffraction peaks at 37.25°, 43.28°, and 62.88° corresponded to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), and (2 2 0) crystal planes of NiO [18], respectively. This result shows that the bivalent NiO is contained in the Fe2O3 sample at the same time; nickel foam after high-temperature treatment contains an oxide layer under air atmosphere. The diffraction peaks at 24.14°, 33.15°, 35.61°, 40.85°, 49.48°, 54.09°, and 63.99° corresponded to (0 1 2), (1 0 4), (1 1 0), (1 1 3), (0 2 4), (1 1 6), and (3 0 0) crystal planes, respectively. The high and narrow diffraction peaks of the resulting product α-Fe2O3 have high crystallinity for α-Fe2O3. With an increasing DMF:water ratio in the electrolyte, the diffraction peaks of α-Fe2O3 became weaker and weaker, indicating that DMF can inhibit the crystal growth. The results of Fe2O3-3 in Fig. 3a showed no α-Fe2O3 generated in the sample, which is consistent with SEM observation in Fig. 2l.

Fig. 3b shows the Raman spectra of C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3-2, C-GQDs, and α-Fe2O3-2. The corresponding characteristic band [19,20] of α-Fe2O3-2 were found at 225, 245, 291, 411, 497, 610, 663, and 1319 cm−1. In the case of C-GQDs, the two bands at 1371 and 1598 cm−1, corresponding to the disordered structural defects (D band) and well-ordered scattering of the E2g phonon of sp2 C atoms (G band), respectively. The intensity ratio of the D and G band is relatively high (ID/IG = 0.9), implying the presence of high density of defects and structure disorder in C-GQDs. For the composites, two characteristic bands at about 1319 (D band) and 1598 cm−1 (G band) can be observed, suggesting the structure of C-GQDs is maintained in the composite. For the composites, the D band shifted about 52 cm−1 towards lower frequency in comparison with C-GQDs. All the fundamental Raman vibrations further evidence that α-Fe2O3 has been combined with the C-GQDs in a perfect way [21]. In addition, the upward shift of the baselines of the composites and C-GQDs is due to the fluorescence effects [22] of C-GQDs indicating the successful combination of C-GQDs and α-Fe2O3-2.

Fig. 4 shows TEM images of C-GQDs. The C-GQDs prepared with Taixi anthracite as the raw material have a uniform size distribution with a diameter of 3.25 ± 1 nm. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image in Fig. 4b shows that the lattice spacing of C-GQDs was 0.20 nm, which is consistent with the literature [23]. Fig. 5 a-b show SEM and EDS images of C-GQDs/Fe2O3-2 composites. Fig. 5a shows that the composite material maintained the antler-shaped skeleton of Fe2O3-2, and the C-GQDs were loaded between the antler-shaped skeletons forming a “bridge effect”. The EDS image also shows that the C-GQDs were loaded on Fe2O3. Due to the well-designed antler-shaped skeleton, composite materials further confirm the solution to the problem of anode. This interesting porous structure was further confirmed by the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis (Fig. S1, Supporting Information), indicative of mesoporous morphology of the C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3-2 with a relatively high BET surface area of 138.4 m2/g. The continuous C-GQDs network can greatly increase the conductivity and protect the numerous pores in the antler-shaped skeleton. The network can effectively buffer the volumetric expansion during charge and discharge. Furthermore, the layer of C-GQDs can also prevent direct contact between electrolyte and Fe2O3, which can form a stable solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film and contribute to the structural integrity of the composite material. The electrochemical performance tests subsequently confirmed the advantages of antler-shaped skeleton structure.

Fig. 6 a shows the cyclic voltammogram of C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3-2 composite—the scan voltage range was from 0.01 to 3 V, and the scan speed was 0.1 mV/s. After high-temperature oxidation treatment, a micron-sized NiO dense active oxide layer was formed on the nickel foam substrate [24,25]. The reduction peak was higher than the oxidation peak of the reverse reaction, indicating that the first cycle will have a large irreversible capacity loss—this is because the electrolyte is decomposed on the electrode surface to form an SEI film. In the first cycle, the reduction peak at 0.4 V was caused by Ni2+ → Ni0(NiO + 2Li+ + 2e- → Ni0 + Li2O), and NiO underwent a lithium intercalation reaction to produce a faradic current. This increases the specific capacitance of the composite material [25]. The peak at 0.6 V corresponds to Fe3+ → Fe0+ to form the SEI film and its side reactions. After the first cycle, the reduction peak at 1.2 V was associated with Ni2+ → Ni0. There were two oxidation peaks at 1.7 V and 2.2 V, respectively. Of these, the 1.7 V peak represents the oxidation peak position of the Fe0 → Fe2+; the 2.2 V corresponded to the further oxidizations of Fe2+ → Fe3+(2FeO + 2Li2O-4e- → Fe2O3 + 4Li+) [26] and Ni0 → Ni2+. The process of SEI film formation is irreversible, and in the second cycle, the reduction peaks originally at 0.4 V and 0.6 V shifted positively to 1.2 V and 0.8 V, respectively. The particles agglomerated in the next few cycles because the lattice structure of Fe2O3 was broken during the process of insertion and extraction of Li+; thus, the Li+ transport rate was smaller than the electron transfer rate, and the position of the reduction peak showed a slightly negative shift. After the second cycle, the intensity of the peaks decreased, and the positions of the reduction peak and oxidation peak remained essentially the same. The CV curves showed a high level of overlap indicating that the C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3-2 composites had excellent reversibility during insertion and extraction of Li+.

Fig. 6b shows the charge-discharge curves of the first seven cycles of the C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3-2 composite at a current density of 0.2 A/g. The charge follows the discharge for a half-cell. The Li+ intercalates into the anode material at the first discharge, and then the formation of the SEI film consumes a large amount of Li+. As a result, the plateau was long, the voltage was low, and the capacity was high—this is consistent with the cyclic voltammogram. During the second cycle, the discharge plateau increased to 1.4 V with a slight downward shift as the number of cycles increased. The charge and discharge specific capacitances of the composite were 1913.9 mAh/g and 2452.6 mAh/g, respectively, and the initial Coulombic efficiency was 78.04%. This may be because the diameter of α-Fe2O3 antler-shaped skeleton in the composite material is smaller and there are more reactive sites. These are conducive to the diffusion of Li+ and the enhancement of electrochemical reaction kinetics. In addition, NiO can provide pseudocapacitance in the charge and discharge of the electrode material to further increase the capacity of the electrode material—this is consistent with the cyclic voltammogram data. The charge-discharge curve exhibited a high degree of overlap with increasing cycles. This shows that the composite electrode has an ideal charge-discharge cyclability.

Fig. 6c shows the cycle performance curves of the composite and Fe2O3 at a current density of 1 A/g. The discharge capacity of the Fe2O3-0 and Fe2O3-1 electrode decreased rapidly. In contrast, the initial discharge capacity of Fe2O3-2 was 2293 mAh/g, and it decayed to 1295 mAh/g after 45 cycles due to the unique antler-shaped skeleton. The cycle performance of the composites was significantly improved versus the Fe2O3-2 electrode. The initial discharge-specific capacitance was 2462.9 mAh/g. After five cycles of charge and discharge, the specific capacitance was 1582.5 mAh/g, and the Coulombic efficiency remained above 98%. The charge and discharge process of the composite material showed good reversibility. After 110 cycles, the specific capacitance was still 1320 mAh/g, and the capacity retention rate was 83.41%. The composite material had better cycling performance because DMF promotes nucleation of α-Fe2O3. More DMF decreases the size of the α-Fe2O3 and increases the specific surface area. It shortens the path to Li+ transport and increases the specific capacitance. The growth of C-GQDs on the surface of α-Fe2O3 effectively prevents the direct contact of α-Fe2O3 with the electrolyte and reduces the irreversible capacity generated during the formation of SEI film from the reaction of α-Fe2O3 with the electrolyte molecules. This increases the Coulombic efficiency. In addition, the incorporation of C-GQDs can improve the volume change and electron transfer efficiency of α-Fe2O3 during insertion and extraction of Li+. This improves lithium storage properties and electrical conductivity.

Fig. 6d shows the rate performance curve of the composite and Fe2O3-2. The capacities of these two electrodes decreased as the current density increased. When the current density was 0.2 A/g or 5 A/g, the specific capacitance of the composite was 1933 mAh/g or 1091 mAh/g. When the current density returned to 0.2 A/g, the specific capacitance of the composite returned to 1851 mAh/g, and the capacity of Fe2O3-2 was only 1382 mAh/g. These data indicate that the introduction of C-GQDs can significantly improve the rate performance of the Fe2O3-2 electrode. In particularly, the return to a small-current charge and discharge after large-current charge and discharge restores the capacity of the composite material to a good level. We concluded that the excellent electrical conductivity of C-GQDs effectively improves the transfer rate and stability of electrons on the surface of the composite material—this improves the rate performance of the composite material.

Fig. 7 compares the AC impedance (electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, EIS) spectra and the corresponding equivalent circuit models of C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3-2 composites and Fe2O3-2 before and after charge and discharge. The AC impedance spectrum is composed of a semi-circle in the high frequency and intermediate frequency regions and a diagonal line in the low frequency region. The resistance intercepted by the intersection of the high frequency region and the real axis is the ohmic resistance value (Rs). This represents the electrolyte impedance and the electrode impedance. The semi-circle of the intermediate frequency region is related to the charge transfer impedance, i.e., Rct representing the charge transfer resistance. The diagonal line in the low frequency region is related to the Li+ diffusion in the electrode material; Q is the double layer capacitance, and ZW is the Warburg impedance [27]. The corresponding parameters could be calculated from the equivalent circuit model as shown in Table 2 . Before the charge and discharge tests, the internal resistance Rs and the charge transfer resistance Rct of the C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3-2 composites were 3.312 Ω and 76.69 Ω, respectively. These were lower than the corresponding values of pure Fe2O3-2 sample (Rs = 2.19 Ω, Rct = 432.7 Ω). The Rct significantly decreased after cycling. The addition of C-GQDs improves the overall conductivity of the material, reduces the charge transfer resistance of the composite material, accelerates the charge transfer process, and increases the Li+ diffusion efficiency. The active material is gradually activated to yield an excellent composite material.

In this paper, C-GQDs were prepared via an acid oxidation method using Taixi anthracite powder as the raw material. The antler-shaped α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles were prepared on a nickel substrate in a controllable way via electrodeposition method by adjusting the electrolyte solvent ratio (VDMF:V water = 2:1). Subsequently, the C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composite material was prepared by two-step electrodeposition with C-GQDs as the electrolyte. The electrochemical test analysis showed that the specific capacitance of C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composites could reach 1582.5 mA/g at a current density of 1 A/g. After 110 cycles, the charge-discharge specific capacitance was still as high as 1320 mAh/g with improved cyclability. In addition, the charge-discharge specific capacitance of C-GQDs/α-Fe2O3 composites could reach 1091 mAh/g at a high current density (5 A/g); this exhibited an ideal rate performance. The excellent lithium storage performance of the composite material can be attributed to the unique antler-shaped structure of α-Fe2O3. Its stable porous skeleton promotes the rapid transport of Li+ while the interconnected C-GQDs in the antler-shaped skeleton exhibits a “bridge effect” that improves the conductive properties of the material. Concurrently, the C-GQDs’ protective layer and numerous porous spaces maintain electrode integrity and buffer volume expansion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We wish to thank Prof Y.T.Z., J.S.Q. and K.B.Z., K.L.J., S.Z.R., G.Y.L., K.K.L., X.Y.L., M.L. for their contributions during the experiment and paper writing process. All authors appreciate the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1703251) and the Key Research and Development Plan of Shaanxi Province (No. 2017ZDCXL-GY-10-01-02) via a Discovery Grant.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fuel.2018.12.030.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
